---
title: "Commuting"
description: "How Canadians get to work, and how long it takes — by city and by mode"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.charts import category_bar, category_bar_views
from pipeline.config import CATEGORICAL_COLORS, CATEGORICAL_OTHER

MAJOR = ["Canada", "Toronto", "Montréal", "Vancouver", "Ottawa - Gatineau", "Calgary",
         "Edmonton", "Winnipeg", "Québec", "Hamilton", "Kitchener - Cambridge - Waterloo",
         "London", "Halifax", "Victoria", "Windsor"]
SHOW = {"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]}
```

How Canadians get to work says a lot about a city's shape. The car dominates almost everywhere, but the largest metros — Toronto, Montréal, Vancouver — carry far more of their commuters on public transit, and the cities built before the car keep higher walking shares. (2021 Census; the pandemic depressed transit use that year, so treat the levels as a recent snapshot rather than a long-run norm.)

## How people commute, by city

```{python}
#| echo: false
mode_path = "data/population/commute_mode.csv"
if os.path.exists(mode_path):
    m = pd.read_csv(mode_path)
    m = m[m["geo"].isin(MAJOR)].copy()
    m["geo"] = m["geo"].str.replace(" - ", "–", regex=False)  # display: en-dash like the rest of the site
    MODE_ORDER = ["Car, truck or van", "Public transit", "Walked", "Bicycle", "Other"]
    MODE_COLOR = {"Car, truck or van": CATEGORICAL_OTHER, "Public transit": CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0],
                  "Walked": CATEGORICAL_COLORS[1], "Bicycle": CATEGORICAL_COLORS[2], "Other": CATEGORICAL_COLORS[3]}
    piv = m.pivot(index="geo", columns="mode", values="share")
    order = piv.sort_values("Public transit", ascending=True).index.tolist()  # most transit at top
    fig = go.Figure()
    for mode in MODE_ORDER:
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(y=order, x=piv.loc[order, mode], name=mode, orientation="h",
            marker_color=MODE_COLOR[mode],
            hovertemplate=f"{mode}: %{{x:.1f}}%<extra>%{{y}}</extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(barmode="stack", plot_bgcolor="white", height=520,
        xaxis=dict(title="Share of commuters (%)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%"),
        yaxis=dict(title=""), legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        margin=dict(l=10, r=20, t=40, b=80),
        annotations=[dict(text="Source: Statistics Canada, 2021 Census, Table 98-10-0457-01.",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.13, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config=SHOW)
else:
    print("Commute-mode data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_commute`.")
```

## How long the commute takes

These are **one-way** times, so a round trip is roughly double. How long that trip takes is longest across the Greater Toronto Area, where many travel between the suburbs and the core. Commuting times dipped when the pandemic kept people home in 2021 and have climbed back since, so these — the most recent figures Statistics Canada publishes — sit above that low. The maroon bar marks the national average.

```{python}
#| echo: false
dur_path = "data/population/commute_duration_lfs.csv"
if os.path.exists(dur_path):
    d = pd.read_csv(dur_path)
    latest = int(d["year"].max())
    d = d[d["year"] == latest]
    fig = category_bar(d, "geo", "avg_min",
        xaxis_title=f"Average one-way commute (minutes), May {latest}",
        value_fmt=".0f", ticksuffix=" min", highlight={"Canada"},
        hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:.1f} min<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Labour Force Survey commuting supplement, May {latest}.")
    fig.show(config=SHOW)
else:
    print("Commute-duration data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_commute_lfs`.")
```

## How long the commute takes, by mode

That all-modes average blends very different journeys. Public-transit riders spend far longer getting to work than drivers — in Toronto, about **48 minutes each way versus 28 by car** — while people who walk or cycle have the shortest trips of all. Use the selector to switch between modes; the maroon bar is the national figure for each. (By-mode commute times are measured by city only in the 2021 Census, so these are one-way 2021 figures; the gap between modes is structural and changes little year to year.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
mode_path = "data/population/commute_duration_by_mode.csv"
if os.path.exists(mode_path):
    dm = pd.read_csv(mode_path)
    dm = dm[dm["geo"].isin(MAJOR)].copy()
    dm["geo"] = dm["geo"].str.replace(" - ", "–", regex=False)  # display: en-dash like the rest of the site
    MODE_ORDER = ["All modes", "Car", "Public transit", "Walked", "Bicycle"]
    views = [(m, dm[dm["mode"] == m]) for m in MODE_ORDER]
    fig = category_bar_views(views, "geo", "avg_min",
        xaxis_title="Average one-way commute (minutes), 2021",
        value_fmt=".0f", ticksuffix=" min", highlight={"Canada"},
        source_note="Source: Statistics Canada, 2021 Census, Table 98-10-0457-01.")
    fig.show(config=SHOW)
else:
    print("Commute-by-mode data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_commute`.")
```

## Download data

- [Commute mode by city (CSV)](../data/population/commute_mode.csv) — 2021 Census, Table 98-10-0457-01
- [Average commute duration by city — current (CSV)](../data/population/commute_duration_lfs.csv) — Statistics Canada, Labour Force Survey commuting supplement
- [Average commute duration by city and mode (CSV)](../data/population/commute_duration_by_mode.csv) — 2021 Census, Table 98-10-0457-01
